topic: An athlete doesn't make the team they expected to join
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the locker room was deafening, amplifying the rhythmic *thud-thud* of my own frustration. I stared at the laminated roster, the name 'Marcus' conspicuously absent from the final lineup. My jaw tightened, the muscle jumping beneath my skin. I picked up my water bottle, the plastic cool against my palm, and took a long, slow drink, letting the coolness try to settle the churning in my stomach. "Seriously?" I muttered, the sound rough in the quiet space. I slammed the bottle down onto the bench, the sharp noise echoing off the metal lockers. I turned, forcing a smile that felt brittle around the edges, and headed toward the exit, every step feeling heavy and deliberate.

[story 2]

I reread the email three times. The polite, corporate language was a thin veneer over the sting of rejection. My hands were clenched so tightly my knuckles were white, and I could feel the tremor running up my forearms. Why? Why was it always the same? I paced the edge of the training facility, the polished floor reflecting my agitated silhouette. I stopped abruptly by the window, watching the distant lights of the city blur. A sharp exhale escaped me, a sound more like a hiss than a breath. "Fine," I whispered to the empty room, the word tasting like ash. I pulled out my phone, intending to call someone, but stopped myself, the impulse to vent feeling too overwhelming to articulate.

[story 3]

The coach’s words still replayed on a loop in my head: 'We need more versatility this year.' Versatility. As if being the best in my specific discipline wasn't enough. I watched the other promising athletes gather around the coach, their easy camaraderie a stark contrast to my solitary feeling of being overlooked. I kicked a loose pebble across the concrete floor, watching it skip aimlessly before it settled. A low growl vibrated in my chest, a deep, internal rumble of sheer disbelief. I straightened my jersey, pulling the fabric taut across my shoulders, and walked toward the exit, my pace quick and jerky, determined to leave the suffocating atmosphere behind.
